exercises.title: The three eras of document AI
exercises.prompt: Documents have layout, tables, diagrams, and structure that plain image understanding misses. Explain the three eras of document AI (OCR pipeline, OCR-free, VLM-native) and what each replaced.
exercises.hint: Era 1: OCR (Tesseract) + LayoutLM + table heuristics — a brittle pipeline. Era 2: OCR-free models (Donut, Nougat) emit structured markup directly from the image. Era 3: VLM-native — feed the page to a high-res VLM (Claude Opus) and get structured output for free.
exercises.solution:
**Why documents need more than image understanding:** a PDF, scientific paper, invoice, or form has **layout, tables, diagrams, footnotes, headers, reading order, and semantic structure** that plain image captioning can't capture. Understanding a document means parsing **where** things are and **how** they relate (this cell belongs to that column, this caption describes that figure), not just 'there's text in the image.'

**The three eras:**
1. **OCR pipeline (pre-VLM):** a **multi-stage pipeline** — **Tesseract OCR** (extract text + coordinates) + **LayoutLMv3** (combine text, layout, and image features for understanding) + **table-extraction heuristics** + other specialized components. *What it was:* the only option before VLMs, but **brittle** — each stage has errors that propagate (OCR mistakes corrupt everything downstream), it needs many components glued together, and heuristics break on unusual layouts. Powerful but fragile and complex.
2. **OCR-free (Donut 2022, Nougat 2023, DocLLM 2023):** **end-to-end models that read the document image and emit structured markup directly** — no separate OCR step. **Donut** maps image → structured output (JSON/markup), **Nougat** maps a scientific-paper image → LaTeX/markdown, **DocLLM** is layout-aware generative. *What it replaced:* the OCR-then-process pipeline — by **going directly from pixels to structure**, OCR-free models **eliminate the OCR error-propagation seam** and the pipeline complexity. One model, image in, structure out.
3. **VLM-native (2026 frontier):** just **feed the page image to a high-resolution general VLM** (Claude Opus 4.7 at 2576px native, Lesson 6) and the **structured-markup output comes for free** — the VLM reads text, parses tables, understands diagrams, and emits structured output as part of its general capability, with **no document-specific model at all**. *What it replaced:* even the specialized OCR-free document models — a strong enough general VLM with high resolution **subsumes** document understanding as a special case of its broad multimodal ability.

**The arc:** **brittle multi-stage pipeline (OCR+LayoutLM+heuristics) → end-to-end OCR-free specialists (Donut/Nougat) → general high-res VLMs that do it for free.** Each era **removed a seam and a specialized component**: OCR-free killed the OCR-error-propagation pipeline; VLM-native killed the need for *any* document-specific model. The throughline is the recurring theme — **a sufficiently capable general model + enough resolution subsumes the specialized pipeline** (the bitter lesson), with **high-resolution native input (Lesson 6) being the enabler** (you can't read dense document text on a square-resized low-res image).

exercises.title: LayoutLMv3 and OCR-free models
exercises.prompt: Describe LayoutLMv3's three input streams (text, layout/bbox, image patches) with unified masking, and compare Donut (OCR-free image→markup), Nougat (paper→LaTeX), DocLLM (layout-aware generative), and PaliGemma 2 (VLM-native). Pick a model for a given document task.
exercises.hint: LayoutLMv3: text tokens + their bounding-box positions + image patches, jointly masked-pretrained. Donut: OCR-free, image→structured JSON. Nougat: scientific PDF→LaTeX. DocLLM: layout-aware generative. PaliGemma 2: general VLM. Pick by task: invoices→Donut/VLM, papers→Nougat, general→VLM.
exercises.solution:
**LayoutLMv3's three input streams (the OCR-pipeline-era SOTA):** it fuses three sources of information about a document:
1. **Text:** the **text tokens** (from OCR) — *what* is written.
2. **Layout (bounding boxes):** each text token's **2D coordinates (bbox)** on the page — *where* it is. This is the key document signal: position encodes structure (columns, tables, headers, reading order).
3. **Image patches:** the **raw visual patches** of the page — fonts, lines, figures, visual style that text+layout alone miss.
These are combined and trained with **unified masking** (mask across all three streams and reconstruct — masked-image-modeling + masked-language-modeling jointly), so the model learns the **relationships between text, position, and appearance**. LayoutLMv3 was the strong OCR-pipeline-era model — but it **still depends on upstream OCR** (text + bboxes come from OCR), so it inherits OCR's error-propagation.

**Comparing the models:**
- **Donut (OCR-free):** **image → structured output (JSON/markup) end-to-end**, no OCR. *Best for:* **forms, invoices, receipts** — structured extraction where you want the fields directly, without an OCR seam. Robust to OCR-failure cases (handwriting, unusual fonts) because it reads pixels directly.
- **Nougat (OCR-free, scientific):** **scientific-paper image → LaTeX/markdown** — specialized for **academic documents** with equations, tables, and complex layout. *Best for:* **scientific papers, technical documents** with math (it reconstructs LaTeX, including formulas, which generic OCR mangles).
- **DocLLM (layout-aware generative):** a **generative model that incorporates layout** (text + spatial info) for document understanding/QA. *Best for:* **layout-heavy document reasoning/QA** where spatial structure matters and you want generative answers.
- **PaliGemma 2 (VLM-native):** a **general VLM** that handles documents as part of broad multimodal ability. *Best for:* **general document tasks** mixed with other vision tasks, or when you want **one model** for everything — and increasingly the default as VLMs get strong/high-res enough.

**Picking a model for a task:**
- **Invoices / receipts / forms (structured extraction):** **Donut** (OCR-free, direct field extraction) or a **high-res VLM** (PaliGemma 2 / frontier VLM) — both avoid OCR errors; VLM if you want flexibility, Donut if you want a tight specialized extractor.
- **Scientific papers (math, LaTeX):** **Nougat** — purpose-built for paper→LaTeX with equations.
- **Handwritten forms / Chinese receipts / unusual fonts:** **OCR-free (Donut) or a strong multilingual VLM** — OCR pipelines fail on handwriting/non-Latin scripts; reading pixels directly is more robust (and the VLM's resolution + multilingual training, Lessons 6-7, helps).
- **General document QA / mixed tasks:** a **VLM-native model** (PaliGemma 2, Claude Opus) — one model, structured output for free, handles diverse documents.
The trend: for most new tasks, reach for a **high-resolution VLM-native model first** (simplest, most general), and use **specialized OCR-free models (Donut/Nougat) when you need a tight, optimized extractor for a specific document type** (invoices, scientific papers) or are compute-constrained. OCR pipelines (LayoutLMv3) are now mostly **legacy**, used where you already have an OCR investment or need its specific outputs.

exercises.title: Why VLM-native won, the resolution dependency, and document RAG
exercises.prompt: Explain why VLM-native document understanding won (resolution, generality, no error-propagation), why it critically depends on high native resolution (Lesson 6), the remaining failure modes (dense tables, tiny text, hallucinated cells), and how this sets up vision-native document RAG (ColPali, Lesson 23).
exercises.hint: VLM-native: one model, no OCR errors, generalizes, structured output free. Needs high resolution (can't read dense text on a low-res square). Failures: very dense tables, tiny fonts, hallucinated table values. Sets up ColPali: embed the page image directly for retrieval (don't extract text at all).
exercises.solution:
**Why VLM-native won:**
1. **No error propagation:** the OCR-pipeline era had a fatal flaw — **OCR errors corrupt everything downstream** (a misread digit in an invoice, a dropped word, a wrong bbox), and the pipeline can't recover. VLM-native **reads pixels directly to structured output in one model**, eliminating the OCR seam — no upstream errors to propagate.
2. **Generality:** a general VLM handles **any document type** (invoices, papers, forms, screenshots, charts, handwriting, any language) **with one model**, vs the OCR era's per-document-type heuristics and the OCR-free era's per-type specialists. It also **reasons about** the document (QA, summarization) not just extracts it.
3. **Structured output for free:** a strong VLM emits **markdown/JSON/structured output** as part of its general capability (Lesson 9's structured output) — no separate parsing stage.
4. **Simplicity:** one model replaces a multi-component pipeline — easier to build, deploy, and improve (the bitter lesson — general + scale beats specialized pipelines).

**Why it critically depends on high native resolution (Lesson 6):** documents are **dense with small text** — a page can have thousands of characters in a small physical area. To **read** that text, the VLM needs **enough resolution that the text is legible in the patch tokens**. On a **square-resized, low-res image** (the pre-2024 default), document text is **squished, blurred, and unreadable** — the model literally can't see the characters (Lesson 6's failure modes). VLM-native document understanding **only became possible** once VLMs supported **high native resolution** (2576px for Claude Opus, AnyRes tiling, NaFlex) — so they can render dense text legibly. **Resolution is the enabler**: no amount of model intelligence helps if the input image is too low-res to read. This is why document AI's VLM-native era coincides with the any-resolution era (Lesson 6) — they're causally linked.

**Remaining failure modes:**
- **Very dense tables:** large, complex tables with many rows/columns can **exceed the model's effective resolution or attention** — it may **misalign cells**, merge rows, or lose track of which value belongs to which column (table structure is hard).
- **Tiny text / fine print:** text below the resolution threshold (footnotes, dense legal fine print, tiny chart labels) is **still unreadable** — even high-res has limits, and tiling can fragment it.
- **Hallucinated cells / values:** like any generative model, a VLM can **hallucinate plausible-but-wrong values** in a table or form — filling in a number that 'looks right' rather than what's actually there (the grounding/faithfulness problem, Phase 11.6). Especially risky for invoices/financial data where exact values matter.
- **Reading order / multi-column:** complex layouts can confuse reading order.
These mean VLM-native isn't perfect — **verification matters** for high-stakes extraction (invoices, medical), and dense/tiny content still challenges resolution.

**How this sets up vision-native document RAG (ColPali, Lesson 23):** the document-understanding lesson is about **reading a single page**. But **retrieval over a corpus of documents** (find the relevant page among thousands) traditionally **extracts text first** (OCR → chunk → embed text), which **loses exactly the signal** this lesson cares about — **layout, tables, figures, fonts, formatting** — and inherits OCR errors. The VLM-native insight ('read the page image directly, structure comes for free') extends to retrieval: **why extract text at all for retrieval?** **ColPali (Lesson 23)** applies the same principle — **embed the page IMAGE directly** (via a VLM like PaliGemma) for retrieval, using late-interaction (ColBERT-style) over **patch embeddings**, keeping all the **layout/figure/formatting signal** that text extraction drops. So this lesson's thesis — **'feed the page image to a VLM and skip text extraction'** — directly motivates **vision-native document RAG**: if VLMs read pages better than OCR pipelines for *understanding*, they should also **index pages better than text-RAG for *retrieval*** (ColPali's 20-40% accuracy gain on visually-rich documents). The progression: **VLM-native understanding (this lesson) → VLM-native retrieval (ColPali, Lesson 23) → cross-modal multimodal RAG (Lesson 24)** — all built on 'don't throw away the visual signal by extracting text.'
